segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: THIRTY SECONDS OF TYPING MAKES A
STRUCTURAL COMMITMENT.
segments.body_html:
You type a command→Forty green checkmarks→A directory you didn't make

The folder appears. The script is done. Now comes the instinct.

beats.text: You open a terminal. You type one command: python new_book.py with two arguments. Thirty seconds later, the terminal stops scrolling. Forty lines of green checkmarks. The last line says done. You list the directory. There is a folder that did not exist a minute ago. Inside: vision.md, architecture.md, chapters-spec.md, risks.md, outline.md, book.md, metadata.yaml, build.sh, README.md. And folders: chapters/, pantry/, images/, d3/, output/, styles/. Forty lines of output representing structural commitments the rest of the book will either keep or betray. The moment the script finishes is the moment you notice what you feel.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE STAKES
title: YOUR INSTINCT SAYS BACK AWAY. THE TRUTH
SAYS OTHERWISE.
segments.body_html:
The instinctThis is someone else's domain · complications I can't see · a door I shouldn't open
⇔
The realityEverything in there you already understand · just with filenames · instead of familiar names

Most authors quit here. They see strangeness and assume complexity. The real obstacle is reading the directory until recognition arrives.

beats.text: When a designer with ten years of client work sees a folder generated by a Python script, the instinct is to back away. The mind says: this is someone else's domain, this is complications I cannot see, this is a door I shouldn't open. The instinct is real. It is also wrong. The only real obstacle the chapter faces is the gap between strangeness and recognition. Once you can see the organizing principle—and there are exactly three audiences in that directory—the rest reads itself. Everything the directory contains is something you already understand. It has a filename instead of a familiar name. Most authors who quit quit at this point not because the task is hard but because they cannot read the directory. They assign complexity to the unknown. The truth is simpler: collapse the strangeness into recognition.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: TWO ARGUMENTS. THAT IS THE ENTIRE
INTERFACE.
segments.body_html:
--tiktocPoints to the TIKTOC.md file from Chapter 4
--slugThe directory name and project identifier

No interactive prompts. No global config file. No choices beyond these two. This is by design.

beats.text: The interface is Audrey Roy Greenfeld's canonical Cookiecutter model: one command, sensible defaults, no global state. Before the script reads anything or creates any files, exactly two arguments enter as prongs. The tiktoc flag points to your TIKTOC.md—the spec document from Chapter 4 containing your chapter list, metadata, arc, and capability statements. The slug is the directory name, the EPUB filename, the URL slug if the book goes online. Kebab-case, lowercase, no spaces: ai-for-designers, not AI for Designers. The interface is deliberately minimal. If you find yourself wanting more flags, Greenfeld's principle suggests you are usually about to make a decision you should have made in TIKTOC.md instead. The script refuses to encode choice into the scaffolder. Your spec makes the choice. The script just materializes it.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE SCAFFOLD IS A TEST YOUR SPEC EITHER
PASSES OR FAILS.
segments.body_html:
Key fact: If TIKTOC.md is missing a chapter title or capability statement, the scaffold reveals it—empty files, blank specs, named gaps.

The first time your spec is held to a deterministic standard is the first time its vagueness becomes visible. The script reads four things: the chapter list, the metadata block, the three-act arc, and the capability statements.

beats.text: The script reads your TIKTOC.md looking for four things: the chapter list, the metadata block, the three-act arc, and the capability statements. These become the scaffold's structural commitments—the empty chapter files waiting to be drafted, the stub specs, the named gaps. The script does not invent anything it doesn't find. If a chapter has no title, the folder appears with an empty file. If a capability is blank, the spec shows blank. This is a feature, not a bug. The first deterministic test your spec encounters is the scaffold. TIKTOC.md either passes or fails. Most authors encounter their first structural inconsistency the moment the scaffold runs—a missing title, a vague capability statement, a three-act arc that does not hold. These become visible not through reading but through generation. The script is a mirror. It reflects back to you exactly what you put in.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE SLUG IS BAKED INTO PATHS AND CANNOT EASILY
CHANGE.
segments.body_html:
1 choice
Hard to change later
→
9 fields
Editable after generation

Every other field in the scaffold is editable. The slug is wired into filenames, paths, and metadata. Choose it as if it will live on a shelf.

beats.text: The slug is the directory name. It becomes the project folder on your machine, the EPUB filename when the book compiles, and the URL slug if the book ever goes online. Kebab-case, lowercase, no spaces: ai-for-designers. Underscore is acceptable but not recommended for web slug compatibility. The metadata block in TIKTOC.md feeds into every structural decision the scaffold makes. Vision statements, architecture notes, risk registers, chapter outlines, book metadata—all editable after the scaffold runs. The slug is different. It is baked into paths. It appears in build commands, in asset directories, in the README. Changing it after generation means hunting through files. You can do it, but you will miss some references. This is why the instruction is absolute: choose the slug once and choose it as if it will live on a shelf for years. Most project decisions are reversible. The slug is not.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: NINE FILES AND FIVE FOLDERS APPEAR WITHOUT
YOUR WRITING THEM.
segments.body_html:
Filesvision.md · architecture.md · chapters-spec.md · risks.md · outline.md · book.md · metadata.yaml · build.sh · README.md
Folderschapters/ · pantry/ · images/ · d3/ · output/ · styles/

Every generated file is a named gap. Every folder has a purpose. None require you to understand the scaffolding machinery.

beats.text: When the script finishes, you have nine new files and five new folders. Vision.md holds your authorial statement—what the book is for and who it is for. Architecture.md describes the structural metaphor and design decisions that will guide chapter construction. Chapters-spec.md lists every chapter with its targets, word count, and status. Risks.md documents known gaps and decision points. Outline.md is the sentence-level breakdown. Book.md collates everything into one rendering-ready document. Metadata.yaml holds technical publication settings. Build.sh is the one-line compilation command. README.md explains what is in the directory. The folders: chapters/ holds the raw Markdown files you will edit. Pantry/ holds reusable reference material and boilerplate. Images/ holds figures. D3/ holds data visualization specifications if needed. Output/ holds compiled artifacts. Styles/ holds CSS for web rendering. None of this requires you to understand Python or scaffolding. It is all intelligible by name. A designer with no programming experience can read this directory.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: IF YOU RUN THE SCRIPT TWICE, NOTHING IS
DESTROYED.
segments.body_html:
First run→Directory created→Second run in same dir→Refused (no overwrite)

The script's guard arc prevents accidental destruction. If you genuinely want to rebuild, delete the directory first. Then rebuild. The refusal is a safety, not a bug.

beats.text: The script refuses to overwrite. If you run it again in the same directory with the same slug, nothing happens. No files are deleted. No changes are made. No overwrites occur. This is a deliberate feature. The script is idempotent in its refusal. If you genuinely want to rebuild the scaffold from scratch—if something has gone wrong or you want a complete reset—delete the directory first. Then run the command again. The scaffold will regenerate. This two-step approach prevents the most common mistake: running a scaffold command twice in succession, not noticing the refusal, and then trying to continue as if the second run had completed. The refusal message is explicit. The philosophy is: it is better to require an explicit delete and re-scaffold than to silently overwrite work-in-progress. The full usage reference, including the exact text of refusal messages, is in Appendix C.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: PYTHON 3.10 INSTALLS IN FIVE MINUTES. ZERO VIRTUAL
ENVIRONMENT FRICTION.
segments.body_html:
MacHomebrew install · brew install python · verify with python3 --version
WindowsPowerShell as Administrator · winget install Python.Python.3.12 · verify with python --version
Linuxapt update && apt install python3 python3-pip · verify both with version flags

No pyenv. No virtual environments. No version managers. Just Python 3.10 or newer, installed to the system PATH.

beats.text: You do not need to learn Python to run this script. You are running one command that someone else wrote so that a directory appears. The installation is inline in this chapter because install friction is a real obstacle. Philip Guo's 2014 analysis of Python adoption at top U.S. universities found one persistent predictor: install friction was the single largest determiner of disengagement in the first week. A designer with professional credentials does not deserve friction on the install. Five minutes. Mac: open Terminal, run the Homebrew installer, run brew install python. Windows: open PowerShell as Administrator, run winget install Python.Python.3.12. Linux: run sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y python3. Each takes roughly five minutes. No Docker, no containers, no configuration. The point is ruthless: you need one script to work. The path from installation to execution should be as short as possible. Friction early is abandonment.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: ONCE YOU SEE THE THREE AUDIENCES IN THAT FOLDER, THE REST
READS ITSELF.
segments.body_html:
Key principle: Start at the top of the directory. There are three audiences in there. Once you can see them, the entire structure becomes transparent.

Every generated file and folder maps to one of three reader types. Understanding which reader each asset serves unlocks the whole organization. This is where strangeness collapses into recognition.

beats.text: The directory is organized around three audiences. The chapter has reached the point of identifying them but not yet elaborating. The principle is this: every file and folder in the scaffold serves one of three reader types. Vision.md and architecture.md are for readers asking what this book is and why. Chapters-spec.md and outline.md are for readers tracking progress and scope. The chapter folders and book.md are for readers who want the actual content. Risks.md and metadata.yaml serve editors and production. Each audience has a path through the directory. Each path shows them what they need and hides what they do not. Most of the confusion authors feel when they first see the scaffold comes from looking at all nine files as if they were equal in importance. They are not. The scaffold is a conversation design. It speaks to different readers at different moments. Once you know who the three audiences are, you know exactly which file to open and what you will find there.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: ONE COMMAND AND ONE SPEC BECOME A COMPLETE DIRECTORY
STRUCTURE INSTANTLY.
segments.body_html:
TIKTOC.md→new_book.py→Generated scaffold→Three audiences

The scaffold translates a plan into named gaps, a directory structure into organized readers, a vague spec into concrete commitments. The moment you type the command is the moment your book's structure becomes locked and visible.

beats.text: The scaffold bridges three moments in the authoring process. First, you have a TIKTOC.md spec—chapter titles, capability statements, metadata, arc. Second, you run one command with two arguments. Third, a directory appears with nine files and five folders, each with a purpose, each speaking to a different reader. The transformation is not magical. The script is deterministic. It reads your spec, validates it, generates files and folders according to fixed rules, and stops. Nothing is invented. Nothing is guessed. What makes the scaffold powerful is that it forces decisions to the surface immediately. The moment the script runs is the moment your vagueness becomes visible as empty files and blank specs. This is a feature. Authors who pass through this moment have a structural commitment they either keep or deliberately break. Authors who back away miss the opportunity to see their spec before they start writing. The scaffold collapses strangeness into recognition through one action: treating your spec as a deterministic input to a known process.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · THE THESIS
title: THIRTY SECONDS LOCKS IN STRUCTURAL COMMITMENT AND COLLAPSES
STRANGENESS INTO RECOGNITION.
segments.body_html:
The scaffold is not code to understand—it is a mirror reflecting your spec, a taxonomy organizing three reader types, and a structural commitment the rest of the book will either keep or betray.

Every instinct to back away from generated code is a signal that your spec contains hidden vagueness. Every empty file and blank spec is a named gap. Reading the directory is the first real draft of your book's structure.

beats.text: The central claim threading through this chapter is that the thirty seconds of typing represents a structural commitment. Not a lock-in that cannot be changed. A commitment that makes subsequent choices either coherent or incoherent with the original plan. Your TIKTOC.md becomes more than a document when it passes through the scaffold. It becomes visible as a set of structural commitments. Some chapters have titles, some do not. Some capability statements are specific, others are blank. Some risks are identified, others are not. The scaffold does not hide this ambiguity. It materializes it as files and folders. This is why the designer's instinct to back away is wrong. The instinct says: this is too complex, too generated, too far from your knowledge. The reality is: this is a mirror showing you exactly what you committed to. Reading the scaffold is the first real draft. Not the first write, but the first read of what you promised to deliver.

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
segments.body_html:
SCAFFOLDING//CODE GENERATION//STRUCTURAL COMMITMENT

AI 1 · Chapter 5 · Book Scaffold: new_book.py
